findings.description: The contract's checkbox-tick rule references a status that the coordinator does not emit. contracts/README.md says `<x or space>: x if issue status is completed, otherwise space`, but the coordinator's friendly statuses (also enumerated in the same contract section just below) are `pending | in_progress | closed | failed` — there is no `completed` status; `completed` is a `close_reason`. The spec gets this right (`- [x]` for `closed` with `close_reason` matching `completed|done`). An implementer following the contract literally would never tick any boxes. The spec-driven test for scenario .1 would catch it, but only after wasted implementation cycles.
findings.suggestion: Replace 'x if issue status is `completed`' with 'x if issue status is `closed` AND `close_reason` matches `completed|done`, otherwise space'. Make the checkbox-tick rule and the status-annotation rule consistent, and align the wording with the spec scenario.

findings.description: The 'blocked on <task_keys>' annotation is required by the contract ('If `depends_on` contains UUIDs whose referenced issues are not closed, append ` — blocked on <task_keys>` to non-closed lines') and shown in the proposal example (`- [ ] T3: smoke test — blocked on T2`), and design.md Open Question 1 even claims 'The spec includes it as part of the status annotation' — but the spec scenario 'Block content reflects current coordinator state' only requires 'the task key, the issue title, and a status annotation showing the assignee (if set) and the completion timestamp (if set)'. It says nothing about blocked-on rendering. The spec is the authoritative artifact, so this requirement is currently un-specified — implementers using only the spec would omit it and the design open question's claim is false.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) add a clause to spec scenario 'Block content reflects current coordinator state' specifying the blocked-on rendering ('AND when an issue's `depends_on` contains UUIDs whose referenced issues are not closed, the line SHALL be suffixed with ` — blocked on <comma-separated task_keys>`'), or (b) defer the blocked-on annotation to v2 by removing it from contracts/README.md and the proposal example. Picking (a) keeps the v1 behavior consistent with the examples; whichever is chosen, also fix design.md Open Question 1 so it accurately reflects the spec.

findings.description: design.md's architecture diagram shows the coordinator surface as `GET /issues?labels=change:...`, but contracts/README.md 'Related coordinator surface' table says `POST /issues/list` (matching `IssueListRequest` in `agent-coordinator/src/coordination_api.py`). Implementers triangulating from these will be confused about which method the bridge issues. Since `try_issue_list` is the only call path, only the bridge needs to know, but the documentation should be coherent.
findings.suggestion: Update the ASCII diagram in design.md to show `POST /issues/list (labels=[change:<id>])` instead of `GET /issues?labels=...`, matching the actual API. Add a one-line note that `try_issue_list` is the call-site abstraction; clients don't construct the HTTP request directly.

findings.description: The hook-test strategy in tasks.md (Phase 4 note) and the work-package description for wp-hooks require the pre-commit / post-merge hooks to respect a `COORDINATOR_TASK_STATUS_RENDERER` environment variable pointing at an alternate script path so tests can substitute a stub. This is a real behavioral requirement of the hooks, and the Phase 4 tests depend on it. But the spec section 'Pre-commit Hook Integration' / 'Post-merge Hook Integration' is silent on it. An implementer reading the spec only would implement the hooks without the env-var override, and the Phase 4 tests would fail.
findings.suggestion: Add a scenario under both 'Pre-commit Hook Integration' and 'Post-merge Hook Integration' along the lines of: 'WHEN the environment variable `COORDINATOR_TASK_STATUS_RENDERER` is set to a script path THEN the hook SHALL invoke that script instead of the default `skills/coordinator-task-status-renderer/scripts/render_tasks_status.py`. This is a test seam.' Then call out in contracts/README.md that this env var is part of the hook contract, not just a test detail.

findings.description: design.md mentions 'short circuit at coordination-bridge timeout (~2s) into stale-marker write' and gives a 'default 5s' hook target, but neither the spec nor the contract requires the renderer (or the bridge it calls) to enforce a timeout. If `try_issue_list` blocks indefinitely (e.g., TCP RST loss), every commit that touches `tasks.md` will hang — a serious UX regression. The fallback behavior is only correct if the renderer actually times out and writes the stale marker.
findings.suggestion: Add a clause to the 'Coordinator-Unreachable Fallback' requirement: 'The renderer SHALL impose a maximum wall-clock time of N seconds (default 5) on its coordinator call; on timeout it SHALL treat the result as a coordinator failure and follow the stale-marker fallback path.' Mirror this in contracts/README.md by adding a `--timeout-seconds` option or documenting the default. Confirm that `coordination_bridge.try_issue_list` accepts a timeout argument or that the renderer wraps it accordingly.

findings.description: The spec mandates 'natural-numeric order (so `1.2` precedes `1.10`)' for line sorting. Real task keys in this change include `2.4a`, `2.9a` (alphanumeric suffix), and the spec acknowledges keys like `T1`, `2.6` (mixed letter-prefixed and dotted-numeric forms). The contract reiterates 'natural-numeric order for dotted keys' without resolving the cross-format question. How should `T1` and `1.1` compare? Where does `2.4a` fall relative to `2.4` and `2.5`? Without a precise algorithm, different implementations (or the same one across patch releases) would order lines differently, defeating the idempotency scenario .3.
findings.suggestion: Specify the comparator precisely. A workable rule: split each key on '.' into segments; for each segment, compare as (integer, suffix) tuples where the leading digit run is the integer (missing → -1) and the rest is the suffix compared lexicographically. Letter-prefixed keys (`T1`) sort by (prefix-string, then trailing-digits-as-int) and rank after all-numeric keys. Add a stable secondary sort (issue UUID) so the renderer is deterministic when keys collide. Add a renderer test fixture exercising `1.1`, `1.2`, `1.10`, `2.4`, `2.4a`, `T1`, `T10` so the comparator is locked down.

findings.description: Tasks 3.1–3.2 ('Write test: /plan-feature Gate 2 approval triggers seeder' and 'Gate 2 re-approval does not duplicate issues') and the wp-plan-feature-integration verification step `pytest skills/tests/plan-feature/test_gate2_invokes_seeder.py` aim to test integration with a SKILL.md-orchestrated step. plan-feature is markdown prose that Claude executes — there is no Python entry point to drive end-to-end from pytest. The realistic test is either a grep over SKILL.md (brittle, doesn't exercise actual orchestration) or an e2e test that requires the agent harness (out of scope for unit tests). Spec scenarios .7/.8 will not be verified by these pytest cases.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) reframe 3.1/3.2 as 'assertion tests on SKILL.md content' (assert the file contains a documented invocation of `seed_tasks_from_md.py` in Step 12 and that the description handles idempotency) and rename the file accordingly, or (b) extract the seeding orchestration into a small Python helper that plan-feature calls, and unit-test that helper. Option (b) is heavier but gives genuine integration coverage. Without one of these, scenarios .7 and .8 are only covered by the wp-integration e2e (5.1), which is fragile to write.

findings.description: The proposal's example block (`- [x] T1: implement foo — done by wp-backend 2026-05-13 (ci/run/4821)`) and the contract's reference example (`done by wp-contracts 2026-05-15 (evidence: ci/run/4821)`) both show an evidence URI suffix, but contracts/README.md explicitly states: 'Evidence URIs are NOT surfaced in v1 (the IssueService does not currently expose a `result.evidence_uri` field on the GET path). Deferred to a follow-up.' A reader will be confused about whether v1 emits the parenthetical or not.
findings.suggestion: Remove `(ci/run/4821)` and `(evidence: ci/run/4821)` from the proposal example and the contracts/README.md 'Managed-Block Format (reference)' example. Note explicitly in a footnote that 'evidence URI rendering is deferred to a future revision.'

findings.description: The contract specifies the status annotation as `done by <assignee> <ISO-date>`, but `<ISO-date>` is not defined. ISO-8601 is a family of formats (full timestamp `2026-05-15T10:42:00Z` vs. date-only `2026-05-15`). The proposal example uses `2026-05-13` (date-only). Without explicit clarity, the renderer test will need to be loose, or the implementation will produce strings that diff against tests across timezones.
findings.suggestion: Replace `<ISO-date>` with `<YYYY-MM-DD>` and add a sentence: 'Dates are emitted in UTC date-only form derived from the issue's closure timestamp. Timezone-converted local dates are not used to keep the output deterministic across hosts.'

findings.description: Spec scenario 'Re-render is idempotent when state has not advanced' guarantees byte-identical output on re-run. The stale-marker emitted on coordinator failure is `> Coordinator unreachable at <ISO-8601 timestamp> — status frozen.` — the timestamp updates on every invocation, so consecutive runs against an unreachable coordinator produce different files. This isn't strictly a contradiction (the precondition mentions 'no intervening coordinator state changes', and an unreachable coordinator may or may not count), but it could cause noisy commit churn during outages because the pre-commit hook re-stages the rewritten file.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) make the stale marker stable by omitting the timestamp or rounding it to a coarse window (e.g., hour bucket) and noting that the marker is 'soft', or (b) add an explicit caveat to the idempotency scenario: 'idempotency does not apply when the renderer is in the coordinator-unreachable fallback path; the timestamp on the stale marker will advance on each run.' Option (a) is the better UX during outages — a quiet sticky marker rather than per-commit timestamp churn.

raw_response.notes: Plan is in good shape overall — D7 (labels-as-identity) and D8 (single-pass topological seeding) are well-justified given the actual coordinator API surface, and D9 (markers-absent + coordinator-unreachable single-write) closes a subtle initialization gap. The two highest-impact issues are f1 (contract specifies a non-existent coordinator status as the checkbox-tick predicate) and f2 (the 'blocked on' annotation is required by the contract and example but not by the spec, which the design.md open-question section incorrectly claims it is). Both are pure documentation fixes; no architectural rework needed. f4 (renderer timeout) and f6 (sort comparator) are worth tightening before implementation to avoid retrofitting tests.
